text:
Radioimmunotherapy (RIT) is emerging as a powerful new approach to cancer therapy; however, tumor delivery of RIT remains a major challenge and current antibodies and ligands also have undesirable pharmacokinetic properties. While small antibody fragments and peptides have superior targeting properties and reduced biodistribution, they accumulate unnecessarily in the kidneys. Nanobodies, which are the smallest antibody fragments (10-15 kDa) that still retain function, have similar propensity to accumulate in the kidneys. In this study we elucidate the reasons behind the renal accumulation of Nanobodies and identified that it is a function of the number of polar amino acids present in the C-terminal amino acid tag.

We investigated the effect of the C-terminus modifications of three nanobody variants on their in vivo behavior. The variants were Myc-His-tagged, His-tagged or untagged and their behavior was studied using dynamic planar imaging in Wistar rats injected with 111In-DTPA labelled nanobodies. The influence of co-infusion of the plasma expander Gelofusin on the kidney accumulation was also evaluated. The relative kidney accumulation of the untagged nanobodies was 30% and 70% lower when compared to the Myc-His-tagged nanobodies at 50 min post injection. All three untagged nanobodies showed a 90% decrease in relative kidney accumulation when co-injected with Gelofusin. In addition, in vivo biodistribution using 177Lu-DTPA-2Rs15d labelled nanobodies on HER2-positive tumour xenograft model in mice confirmed the effectiveness of using untagged nanobodies with 95% decrease in kidney accumulation without affecting the tumour accumulation.

Dosimetry for the untagged 177Lu-DTPA-2Rs15d with Gelofusin was determined to be equivalent in both the tumor (0.90 Gy/MBq) and kidneys. Other organs were minimized in radiation exposure. With delivering 6 times more radiation to the tumor than 177Lu-DTPA-Trastuzumab, 177Lu-DTPA-Trastuzumab led to higher radiation exposure in lung, liver, spleen, bone and blood (by factors ranging from 26 to 4180). Radiolabeled Trastuzumab nanobodies for Therapy of Mouse Models of Aggressive HER2+ Tumors The nanobody-based RIT led to nearly complete tumor growth inhibition (P<0.0001) and event-free survival. There was no indication of renal inflammation, apoptosis or necrosis in histological analysis of kidney tissues at 45days after injection.

Our studies reveal that the C-terminal amino acid composition is critical for the renal accumulation of the molecules. By optimizing the C-terminal amino acid composition, we have developed a protocol to improve the therapeutic index of radiolabeled nanobodies and thus provide a highly promising tool for the treatment of metastatic and minimal residual disease.

prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
